headline_of_the_day: Federal judge blocks Paramount-Warner Bros merger on antitrust grounds

key_points.text: A judge paused the Paramount-Warner Bros merger for 14 days while states argue it breaks antitrust law.
Hackers are attacking two critical WordPress bugs that were just patched, risking tens of millions of websites.
A Craneware breach exposed billing data from thousands of US hospitals and pharmacies.
The EU fined AliExpress 550 million euros for selling unsafe toys and counterfeit goods.

stories.title: LG monitors silently install McAfee ads through Windows Update
stories.summary: Plugging in a new LG UltraGear display automatically downloads an app installer without asking first.

article.background: When you plug in a new LG UltraGear monitor to a Windows 11 PC, the system runs Windows Update. This update silently installs LG driver software and an app installer. Neither one asks for your permission first.
article.what_happened: The LG Monitor App Installer, once on your computer, starts showing pop-up ads. These ads promote LG's other apps and McAfee antivirus. The app has no controls of its own. It simply pushes notifications to your screen without your consent.
article.why_it_matters: You buy a monitor to see your screen better, not to see ads. The installer arrives hidden inside a Windows Update, so most people won't know it is there. You get pop-ups from a tool you never asked for, promoting software you may not want.

stories.title: Complete technical guide explains how New York subway signals work
stories.summary: A guide explains the color-light block signaling system used across all NYC subway lines.

article.background: The NYC subway uses a signal system called wayside color-light block signalling. Signals sit beside the track. They use colored lights to show train operators if the track ahead is clear. The track is split into blocks, or sections. The system detects when a train is in each block.
article.what_happened: A guide documents how this signal system works across all NYC subway divisions. It explains the principles behind the colored lights and track blocks. The system's basic design has stayed the same for decades.
article.why_it_matters: Understanding how subway signals work helps riders grasp why trains slow down or stop. It shows how the system keeps trains safely spaced apart. Knowing this makes transit delays easier to understand.

stories.title: Chinese lab Moonshot releases Kimi K3 model with open weights coming
stories.summary: Moonshot announced a 2.8 trillion parameter model and plans to release its weights on July 27.

article.background: Moonshot AI, a Chinese research lab, released Kimi K3 on July 16th. K3 is a large language model with 2.8 trillion parameters. A parameter is a number that helps an AI system make decisions. Moonshot plans to release the model's weights, or underlying code, on July 27th. Open-weight models let anyone see and use the code, unlike closed models that companies keep private.
article.what_happened: Kimi K3 became the largest open-weight language model available. The release sparked debate about AI's future. Some people, including OpenAI's Dean W. Ball, worry that open-weight models hurt profits for major AI companies. Others, like researcher Yann LeCun, argue that open code helps innovation and can exist alongside paid systems.
article.why_it_matters: If Moonshot releases K3's weights as promised, smaller groups can build AI tools without paying for expensive systems. This could shift power in the AI industry away from wealthy companies. The debate also reflects a deeper question: whether AI should be open for everyone to use or controlled by large labs. Your ability to use AI tools may depend on which approach wins out.

stories.title: AI solves 20-year-old graph theory conjecture with proof
stories.summary: Researchers used AI to prove that a greedy algorithm works best for semi-streaming matching.

stories.title: Federal judge blocks Paramount-Warner Bros merger on antitrust grounds
stories.summary: A judge stopped the deal for 14 days after states argued it would raise prices and cut content choices.

article.background: Paramount and Warner Bros. Discovery are two major studios that make movies and TV shows. A group of twelve state attorneys general, led by California, sued to block their $110 billion merger. They argue it would reduce competition and harm consumers.
article.what_happened: A federal judge granted a 14-day pause on Monday. Judge Araceli Martínez-Olguín said the states showed serious legal questions about antitrust violations. The pause gives both sides more time before the merger can close. Paramount had already agreed not to finish the deal before July 22.
article.why_it_matters: The states worry the combined company could raise prices and produce fewer movies and shows. A bigger studio with less competition can control more of what gets made. The judge found the states' argument strong enough to halt the deal at least temporarily while the case continues.

stories.title: EU fines AliExpress record 550 million euros for illegal goods
stories.summary: The EU says AliExpress failed to stop sales of unsafe toys and counterfeit goods.

article.background: AliExpress is a Chinese online marketplace where sellers offer goods to buyers worldwide. The EU has rules requiring online platforms to check for illegal or unsafe items. These include counterfeit clothes, fake toys, and dangerous cosmetics. The European Commission enforces these rules through the Digital Services Act.
article.what_happened: The European Commission fined AliExpress €550 million (£467 million) for failing to stop illegal sales. Officials found AliExpress did not hire enough staff to catch bad products. Moderators had only tens of seconds to check flagged items. The company also made it easy for sellers to hide illegal goods.
article.why_it_matters: When online platforms fail to police what they sell, shoppers buy fake or unsafe things without knowing. This fine sets a clear rule: platforms must work to stop illegal sales or face big penalties. If AliExpress now hires more staff to check products, your risk of getting counterfeits or unsafe items drops.

stories.title: Hackers exploit WordPress bugs affecting tens of millions of sites
stories.summary: WordPress patched two critical bugs last week, but hackers are already attacking unpatched sites.

article.background: WordPress is the software behind about 40 percent of all websites. Last week, WordPress fixed two critical security flaws that could let hackers take over sites. The company told users to update "immediately" and even turned on forced updates.
article.what_happened: Hackers are now breaking into WordPress sites that still run the old, vulnerable version. Three security firms, Patchstack, Hexastrike, and WatchTowr, all warned that attacks are happening right now. Tens of millions of WordPress websites remain at risk as of Monday.
article.why_it_matters: If your website runs WordPress, you need to update it now. Any unpatched site could be hijacked to steal data, spread malware, or host spam. The longer you wait, the higher the chance attackers will find your site.

stories.title: Healthcare vendor Craneware confirms data breach affecting thousands of hospitals
stories.summary: Craneware, a UK billing software maker, says hackers stole customer data from US medical facilities.

article.background: Craneware is a U.K.-based company that makes billing and accounting software. Thousands of U.S. clinics, hospitals, and pharmacies use its software to handle their finances and patient billing. This software processes sensitive financial and patient data.
article.what_happened: Hackers stole a significant amount of data from Craneware's systems, the company announced Monday. The stolen data included employee records, customer information, and partner records. Craneware said the hackers have been removed from its systems. The investigation is still ongoing.
article.why_it_matters: Because so many U.S. medical facilities rely on Craneware, the breach affects a large number of hospitals, clinics, and pharmacies. Stolen patient and financial data puts those facilities and their patients at risk. Healthcare providers may face costs to respond to the breach and notify affected patients.

stories.title: Malware hides stolen files in Microsoft 365 calendar events dated 2050
stories.summary: The HollowGraph tool hides stolen data inside hijacked calendars to blend in with normal Microsoft activity.

article.background: Microsoft 365 is a cloud service millions of organizations use for email, calendars, and documents. Security tools usually watch for unusual traffic to servers owned by attackers. But a new malware called HollowGraph uses a different hiding spot.
article.what_happened: Researchers at Group-IB found malware that hijacks a Microsoft 365 calendar to receive orders and send stolen files. The malware plants instructions and data on calendar events dated to 2050. It moves everything through normal Microsoft Graph API traffic, which your company's security system sees as routine Microsoft activity.
article.why_it_matters: Most malware gets caught because it phones home to attacker servers. HollowGraph avoids that. It hides inside a service your organization already trusts and uses every day. This means attackers may steal data from your company without triggering the usual alarms.
